items.q: Why does first ionisation energy generally increase across a period?
items.correct: Nuclear charge increases while shielding stays roughly constant, so electrons are held more tightly.
items.misconception: Atoms get bigger across a period, making electrons easier to add but harder to remove.
items.random_wrong: Atoms across a period have more shells, pulling electrons closer.
items.why: Students confuse the direction of the atomic-radius trend across a period (it actually decreases) and use wrong size reasoning.

items.correct: Delocalised electrons are free to move through the lattice of positive ions, carrying charge.
items.misconception: Metal atoms themselves move to carry the current.
items.random_wrong: Metals contain naturally moving protons that act as charge carriers.
items.why: Students picture conduction as 'something moving through the wire' and default to whole atoms, since they don't fully grasp delocalisation.

items.q: Where is almost all the mass of an atom located?
items.correct: In the nucleus, made up of protons and neutrons.
items.misconception: Electrons contribute significantly to the mass of an atom.
items.random_wrong: Mass is spread evenly throughout the entire atom including the electron shells.
items.why: Students see electrons listed alongside protons and neutrons and assume they contribute comparable mass — in reality an electron has ~1/1836 the mass of a proton.
The answer examiners want is: Cl2 + 2KI -> 2KCl + I2. “Br2 + 2KCl -> 2KBr + Cl2” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which is NOT a typical transition metal property?
items.correct: Soft and very reactive with water
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Soft and very reactive with water. “High melting point” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Iron forms two common ions. The smaller positive charge of these is +2. What is the larger?
items.why: Exact answer: 3. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
